01
NAMMA BENGALURU

Chapter 01 — Perspective

WHERE
IT ALL
BEGAN

ನಮ್ಮ ಊರು, ನಮ್ಮ ಜನ, ನಮ್ಮ ಮನೆ — Our city, our people, our home.

Growing up in a joint family in Bengaluru means you never really eat alone, you never really celebrate alone, and you definitely never have a bad day without at least four people asking you what's wrong. I wouldn't trade it for anything. That sense of showing up for people — that stuck.

Music is the other constant. Not just making it — though I've done plenty of that — but the way a good song can walk into the room and completely change it. Hip-hop, Indian classical drifting from the next flat, a set that starts at midnight and somehow ends at 4am. I've always been more obsessed with why music hits than how it's made. Turns out that's not that different from building products.

Tempe, Arizona is about as different from Bengaluru as a place can be — dry heat instead of monsoons, wide roads instead of traffic that has its own personality, and genuinely no one honking. Still getting used to that last one. But the values, the music obsession, and the hunger? Fully packed in the carry-on.

02
Click to download ↓

Chapter 02 — The Playbook

THE PM
INTERVIEW
PLAYBOOK

I spent months prepping for PM interviews and noticed most guides were either too shallow or too abstract to be useful in the room. So after clearing my Microsoft interview, I wrote the one I wish someone had handed me on day one. 8 chapters. Real frameworks. The mental models that actually held up under pressure. Free — because gatekeeping knowledge is the one thing I genuinely can't stand.

01

"Fall in love with the problem, not the solution. Features are hypotheses — users are the truth."

02

"The best PM in the room is rarely the loudest. They're the one who reframes the question."

03

"Metrics without a narrative are just numbers. Tell the story the data is trying to say."

01The Fundamentals of PM Interviews
02Behavioural Interviews — STAR+L FrameworkMost Read
03Product Design & Sense — STEP Framework
04Strategy — Porter's 5 Forces & Build/Buy/Partner
05Execution & Metrics
06Technical & AI PM Interviews
07Take-Home Assignments & FAANG Specifics
08The Final Countdown — Recruiter & Closing

03

Chapter 03 — Craft & Collaboration

MECHANICAL
MIND

Four years of Mechanical Engineering at Reva University, Bengaluru (2018–2022). One thing Reva drilled into me: a clean design isn't about elegance for its own sake — it's about respect. Respect for the person who has to maintain it, fix it at 2am, or hand it off to someone new. That philosophy followed me from CAD drawings to product specs.

Between labs and late submissions, I was organising events, writing music, and figuring out that the best systems — mechanical or social — are the ones people actually want to use. Engineering gave me the discipline to ask "will this hold?" The campus gave me the freedom to ask "should we build this at all?" That combination turned out to be the whole setup.

3.5
Foundation
Reva University
4.0

Years Building

04

Chapter 04 — The Movement

THE STREETS
TAUGHT ME
RHYTHM

Engineering was building my resume. Hip-hop was building the rest of me. I co-founded Seedhseedha Hip-hop with people I trusted — started as a bunch of us rapping in car parks, grew into a real collective with gigs, sponsors, and brand campaigns. Then we helped scale Soul Jams into a movement across 4 cities. That 'we' never gets old.

Shows. Cyphers. Writing bars at midnight between assignment deadlines, telling myself I'd sleep after one more verse (I never did). Hip-hop wasn't a side project — it was the place I learned what it actually takes to hold people's attention. That's a skill that transfers.

ASU Show · August
Soul Jams · Bengaluru
Soul Jams Community
Seedhseedha Crew
"Whether I'm on a stage or in a boardroom — the goal is the same: finding the rhythm in the chaos."
05
Best Employee Q4 2024 + Q2 2025

Chapter 05 — Systems & Scale

PRODUCT
MANAGER
@ ACCENTURE

Dec 2022 – Jul 2025. Healthcare software is a funny space — the users are nurses and doctors who have about thirty seconds to interact with your interface before moving on to something that actually matters. That pressure made me a better PM. I led a 25-person cross-functional team, wrote more SQL than I'd care to admit, and shipped modules that quietly kept Kaiser Permanente hospitals running. Won Best Employee twice — which I think mostly means I replied to Slack fast. The app was happening in parallel. Sleep is overrated.

  • Helped clinical teams recover 20% processing time — time they gave back to patients
  • Built automation that cut reporting latency 60% — nobody had to run that manually again
  • 1,500+ operational reports — each one a decision someone didn't have to guess at
  • Automated 10K+ monthly records — gave a whole team back 6 hours every week
  • Mentored 20+ interns — the part of the job I'd do for free
Default modeCross-functional by default. I doc so others don't have to guess.
Problem framingUser pain → metric → hypothesis → test
StakeholdersI write the brief, I run the retro
When stuckTalk to a user. Every time, without exception.

What colleagues say

"Amit doesn't just ship features — he asks why we're building them in the first place. That question saved us weeks on the wrong thing."

— Colleague, Accenture / Kaiser Permanente project

"He was my first mentor at Accenture. The reason I stayed for year two. Genuinely one of those people who makes everyone around them better."

— Former intern, Accenture Bengaluru
06

Chapter 06 — The Builder

BUILDING
CIRCLES IN

Circles IN started the way all good things start — a school friend, Sathvik C, and a conversation over chai that neither of us walked away from. He brought the vision. I brought the product thinking. We built it together, evenings and weekends, while both of us had full-time jobs. Mar 2024 – Jul 2025. It ended up with real users doing real things — which still surprises me a little.

Amit & Sathvik C · Two school friends, one big idea, zero hesitation 🤝
50K+
Monthly Active Users
65%

Wait Time Reduced via QR Check-ins
55%

07
☁ Microsoft · May 2026

Chapter 07 — What's Next

READY FOR
MAY 2026.
REDMOND.

At Microsoft, scale isn't just a number — it's a responsibility. It's the realisation that a single button I design or a logic flow I build affects the daily rhythm of millions of people. My time here has taught me that true "Enterprise" work isn't about being cold or rigid; it's about being incredibly reliable. I spend my days solving puzzles at the intersection of complex legacy systems and future-facing AI, figuring out how to make "Big Tech" feel personal and supportive. It's where the grit of Bengaluru meets the precision of Redmond.

PM Intern · Redmond, WA ASU · GPA 4.0 ISD Marketing Head Tech Consultant · ASU Six Sigma Black Belt

Connect

LET'S
CONNECT

If you found this through the PM Playbook, a LinkedIn rabbit hole, or a mutual who said 'just talk to him' — hey. I genuinely love connecting with people who are building things, questioning things, or just figuring things out. Reach out. The best conversations always start a little sideways.
